data.title: Trump's 50-Year Mortgage Idea Could Be A Big Gift To REITs
data.description:
It was recently reported that the Trump administration is working on a new 50-year mortgage proposal to make homeownership more affordable.

Bill Pulte, director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, commented that this is a "complete game-changer" for the housing industry.

It has the potential to reduce monthly average payments, increasing the affordability of homeownership for the masses.

Pulte added that this is just one proposal among many others to increase the affordability for young home buyers. Here's what he said:

We are laser focused on ensuring the American Dream for young people and that can only happen on the economic level of homebuying. A 50-year mortgage is simply a potential weapon in a wide arsenal of solutions that we are developing right now. Stay tuned!

This news has led many of you to ask me how this could potentially impact REITs if this becomes a done deal.

The answer is that it depends on the property sector, but overall, I would expect it to benefit a lot more REITs than it hurts.

Therefore, I view this as a gift to REIT investors.

Here's a step-by-step review going through individual property sectors. Those not mentioned likely won't be significantly impacted:

Single-Family Rental REITs: Positive Impact

Government-subsidized 50-year mortgages have the potential to make homeownership more affordable, increasing the demand for housing and pushing home prices to even higher levels.

This could benefit single-family rental REITs, like Invitation Homes (INVH) and American Homes 4 Rent (AMH), by increasing their portfolio net asset values. It would also potentially remove or at least mitigate the fears of a housing crash, which has hurt their market sentiment in recent years.

Moreover, the higher portfolio net asset values would also allow them to accelerate their capital recycling plans, selling homes in the private market in order to develop new communities, earning large spreads over their cost of capital.

But on the other hand, it could also reduce the demand for rentals, slowing down future rent growth. The good news is that occupancy rates are very high and rent growth is solid right now at 3-5%, so even with a dip in demand, the fundamentals should remain relatively strong.

I think that the net impact would be positive for these REITs, increasing their property values, accelerating external growth, and boosting market sentiment, at the cost of slightly lower rent growth.

Invitation Homes is today priced at one of its lowest valuations ever, trading at an estimated 40% discount to its net asset value, so the upside could be significant if you remove the fear of a housing market crash.

Self-Storage REITs: Very Positive Impact

This is likely the biggest winner of this new change.

Self-storage REITs, like Public Storage (PSA), Extra Space Storage (EXR), and CubeSmart (CUBE), suffered from weak demand in recent years because few people were moving around.

When housing is unaffordable, transaction activity slows down, and people stay put, reducing the demand for self-storage space.

But if the 50-year mortgage is now introduced, it could spark a new wave of homebuying, which would likely lead to a spike in new demand for self-storage as more people move from one home to another.

This has historically been one of the strongest demand drivers for self-storage, so the impact could be significant.

Today, self-storage REITs are priced at exceptionally low valuations due to the weak demand currently slowing down their growth. This means that the sector offers significant upside potential as demand potentially accelerates from here, in part thanks to the new 50-year mortgage.

Specialty REITs: Positive Impact

There are also a few specialty REITs that could benefit.

More demand for housing would also lead to more construction activity, which has historically resulted in more demand for lumber, increasing its prices. This then benefits timberland REITs, which harvest a portion of their trees each year.

Higher commodity prices then flow straight to the bottom line, resulting in higher cash flow and dividend payments.

Secondly, if this leads to more construction activity, it could also lead to more better use conversion projects, selling timberland to housing developers.

Rayonier (RYN) has noted that such conversion projects can increase the value of the land 10-15x:

Farmland REITs like Farmland Partners (FPI) could also benefit from such better-use conversion projects to build more housing:

Finally, the newly listed Millrose Properties (MRP) could also be a big winner since it owns primarily homesites that are waiting to be sold or developed.

It's a recent spin-off from the homebuilder, Lennar (LEN), which still owns 20% of its equity, and has option contracts to buy home sites off Millrose over time.

It's essentially the only "land banking REIT," and it could win a lot from greater housing affordability.

Retail REITs: Positive Impact

A lot of retail REITs own neighborhood strip centers in growing suburbs of Sunbelt markets. These properties are often surrounded by single-family housing communities and ample land that's zoned for additional housing.

Therefore, these retail properties are set to indirectly benefit from the growing demand for single-family houses, as it could lead to greater population density, resulting in higher tenant sales and faster long-term rent growth.

Typically, with growing demand and ample available land, the market would quickly respond by building more retail centers to meet the demand, but this seems unlikely because retail rents are still too low to justify new development projects.

According to a study by Kimco (KIM), retail rents would need to rise by 65% on average in its markets to make new development projects economically feasible.

This explains why few new retail centers are getting built today, despite the high occupancy rates and growing demand.

It also means that rent growth is likely to continue at a solid clip for many years to come in these growing markets, and this growth could accelerate even further if we get 50-year mortgages.

Apartment REITs: Negative Impact

For apartment REITs, the net impact would likely be negative.

It would weaken demand, putting pressure on occupancy rates and future rent growth. Right now, the apartment sector is already oversupplied, so weaker demand would only make things worse.

It could also hurt the sector's market sentiment, resulting in higher cap rates and lower property values.

The good news is that the supply outlook will improve significantly in 2026 and even more so in 2027, which should make up for a potentially weaker demand.

The net impact would likely still be negative, but not that significant.

Bottom Line

Overall, if the government steps in to further subsidize the housing sector, this should benefit single-family rental REITs, self-storage REITs, timberland REITs, and certain retail REITs, but it could potentially hurt apartment REITs.

As such, it should benefit many more than it hurts, leading to higher cash flows and property values in many different sectors. At High Yield Landlord, we're closely monitoring this new development and will adapt our portfolio allocation accordingly.

data.title: Comstock Holding Remains Attractively Valued, Despite Its Spectacular Rally
data.description:
Comstock Holding Companies (CHCI) has rallied 66% this year thanks to its strong business performance and its promising growth prospects. The stock has outperformed the broad market by an eye-opening margin in almost any time horizon one can examine. For instance, it has outperformed the S&P 500 this year (+66% vs. +13%) and over the last five years (+364% vs. +85%). After such striking outperformance, it is only natural that some investors may hesitate to buy Comstock Holding, thinking that the stock has become overvalued and overripe for reversion to the mean. However, this is far from true. The stock remains attractively valued and still has ample room for future growth. Therefore, investors should not dismiss the stock.

Business Overview

Comstock Holding was founded 40 years ago and operates as an asset manager, developer, and operator of mixed-use and transit-oriented properties in Washington, D.C. It offers its customers a broad range of real estate services, such as property management, construction management, development, parking management, and security.

The vast majority of the companies that focus on real estate, such as REITs, carry significant amounts of debt. However, this is not the case for Comstock Holding, which has a rock-solid balance sheet thanks to its strong business model.

The company operates with a fee-based, asset-light, debt-free business model, which drives consistent revenue growth and offers resilience during recessions. In addition, the long-term asset management contracts secure reliable revenues and visibility into future earnings.

The long-term 2022 Asset Management Agreement, which covers the most significant properties of Comstock Holding, has generated 23% average annual growth of revenue and 35% annual growth of adjusted EBITDA over the last five years. Even better, the company does not rest on its laurels and has a promising development pipeline in place.

As shown above, Comstock Holding is currently operating 91 assets and has another 4 assets under construction as well as 11 assets under development. It is thus likely to remain in growth mode in the upcoming years.

Comstock Holding enjoys positive business momentum this year. In the first nine months of this year, the company reported a 13% increase in revenue over the prior year's period. Notably, it has grown its year-to-date revenue for 27 consecutive quarters, including 25 quarters with double-digit growth. The strong performance in the first nine months of this year resulted primarily from fee-based revenue from management subsidiaries, which surged 37% over the prior year's 9-month period.

Net income decreased 15%, but the decrease was caused by some one-time operating expenses related to the ParkX subsidiary. These costs were related to the setup of a new porter/janitorial service offering. When these costs revert to normal, the top-line growth is likely to result in growth in the bottom line as well.

Balance Sheet

Comstock Holding has a pristine balance sheet. Not only does the company abstain from debt issuance, but it also has a net cash position. As per Buffett's formula (net cash = cash + receivables - total liabilities), Comstock Holding has net cash of $28 million. This amount is 22% of the market capitalization of the stock, and hence it is undoubtedly significant.

Thanks to its rock-solid balance sheet, Comstock Holding does not pay any interest expense. Instead, it has earned net interest income of $0.8 million in the last 12 months. The pristine balance sheet of the company is a testament to the strength of its business model, which does not require the issuance of debt to fund future growth. Very few companies can grow without the use of any debt. In addition, the strong financial position of Comstock Holding renders the company somewhat resilient to downturns, in contrast to other companies, which struggle to service their debt during rough periods.

Valuation

Comstock Holding is currently trading at a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of 9.6x, which is lower than the earnings multiple of most of its peers. To provide a perspective, The RMR Group (RMR) and Sun Hung Kai Properties (OTCPK:SUHJY) are trading at price-to-earnings ratios of 14.8x and 15.2x, respectively, whereas AMREP (AXR) is trading at a price-to-earnings ratio of 8.0x.

Moreover, Comstock Holding has one of the best current ratios in its peer group and a net cash position, in contrast to most of its peers, which have some debt in their balance sheets. As mentioned above, Comstock Holding has a net cash position that is equal to 22% of its current market capitalization. If the net cash position is subtracted from the stock price, then the stock is trading at a price-to-earnings ratio of only 7.5x (=9.6*0.78). To cut a long story short, despite its spectacular rally, Comstock Holding remains attractively valued.

Long-term Outperformance

Comstock Holding has outperformed the S&P 500 in almost any time horizon one can examine. To be sure, the stock has massively outperformed the benchmark over the last decade (+573% vs. +216%), over the last five years (+364% vs. +85%), and over the last 12 months (+44% vs. +12%). Very few companies can boast of outperforming the S&P 500 with such great consistency. The consistent outperformance reveals the presence of an exceptionally strong business model and high-quality management with a long-term perspective.

Risks

As Comstock Holding engages in the real estate industry, it is inevitably sensitive to the underlying conditions in the economy and in the housing market. Whenever a recession or a housing crisis shows up, the stock of Comstock Holding is likely to come under pressure. The fee-based model and the long-term agreements of the company with its customers mitigate its sensitivity to economic crises, but still the company is not immune to downturns.

The other risk factor has to do with the aforementioned elevated operating costs reported in the most recent earnings report. These costs caused a 15% decrease in net income, despite a steep increase in revenues. While management stated that a portion of these costs is non-recurring in nature, this remains to be seen in the next earnings report, in February. The 66% rally of the stock this year signals that the investing community agrees on management's view.

Finally, Comstock Holding has a market capitalization of only $129 million, and hence it is a small-cap stock. Small-cap stocks are considered riskier than large-cap stocks, as they have less firepower to endure a potential prolonged downturn due to their small size. On the bright side, the healthy balance sheet of Comstock Holding increases the resilience of the company to crises.

Final Thoughts

When most investors think about real estate stocks, they think about REITs, which often carry material amounts of debt. This is not the case for Comstock Holding, which has a net cash position thanks to its reliable, fee-based model. The company has 40 years of expertise in its business and has proved capable of outperforming the S&P 500 over the long run. Only the best-of-breed companies can boast of such an accomplishment. Even better, despite its outperformance, the stock remains attractively valued, and hence it is likely to continue rewarding investors with a long-term perspective.

data.title: Artisan Global Discovery Fund: Q3 Delivers Strong Results Amid Trimming And Exits
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Artisan Global Discovery Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

The portfolio generated a modestly positive absolute return in Q3, but it trailed the MSCI All Country World Small Mid Index. The Q3 underperformance was driven by stock selection in the IT, financials and consumer discretionary sectors.

Amid the market excitement surrounding AI, our semiconductor, hardware and equipment names, such as Pure Storage (PSTG), Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) and Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), were strong contributors to the portfolio's relative performance. However, our software industry holdings, including Tyler Technologies (TYL) and Atlassian (TEAM), were a significant drag. Given the rapid advancements in AI capabilities, investors have become more uncertain about which software and services companies can successfully integrate AI into their products and which will have their business models severely disrupted.

Within financials, recent market and economic conditions have been more favorable for banks and other businesses that are credit-sensitive and spread-based. However, as a quality-focused growth manager, these are businesses that just won't typically meet our investment criteria.

On the positive side, stock selection in the health care and industrials sectors was the strongest contributor to performance. Some of the earlier fears over pharmaceutical tariffs and US drug price controls appear to have moderated. Still, the success within health care, the portfolio's largest sector exposure, was driven by strong company-specific results, particularly among our biotech holdings.

Health care is still not a popular sector for investors, hindered by policy uncertainty and understandably overshadowed by investor fascination with all things AI. However, we always say that where there's innovation, you tend to find profit cycles. And plenty of innovation is still occurring within health care.

Among our top contributors were Modine Manufacturing (MOD), Pure Storage and Ascendis Pharma (ASND). Modine is a global leader in thermal management solutions. The company is experiencing strong growth, driven by increased business with existing hyperscale customers and the addition of new accounts in the hyperscale segment. Rising demand across newer geographies, particularly in the US and Southeast Asia, and Modine's expansion into advanced product lines, such as rack-level and modular data center cooling solutions, have further fueled this momentum. As a result, the company has significantly raised its revenue expectations for the year.

Pure Storage is redefining enterprise data storage by sourcing raw NAND flash memory and building custom all-flash systems, which enhances cost efficiency. The company is gaining share in core enterprise markets, and its collaboration with SK hynix for hyperscale data center environments supports its efforts to expand its addressable market. During the quarter, Pure Storage reported revenue rose 13% year over year to $861 million, driven by enterprise demand and strong performance in its subscription services and next-generation product portfolio. While cloud and hyperscaler traction are still early, core business metrics remain solid.

Ascendis is a biotech company leveraging its proprietary TransCon™ platform to develop differentiated therapies that address unmet medical needs. This technology enables sustained and predictable drug release while preserving the drug's original mechanism of action. The launch of Yorvpath®—the first and only hormone replacement therapy for adult hypoparathyroidism—has exceeded expectations, generating revenues of €103 million in Q2 2025, more than double the prior quarter, with over 3,100 patients and 1,500 US prescribers. This strong uptake reinforces our confidence in both Yorvpath® and Ascendis' broader pipeline.

Among our Q3 detractors were London Stock Exchange Group (LNSTY), PROCEPT BioRobotics (PRCT) and Wingstop (WING). London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG) is a leading global provider of market infrastructure and capital markets services. We are attracted to its ability to further monetize its proprietary data, particularly through its early partnership with Microsoft (MSFT). During the quarter, competitive pressures intensified in the Workstation segment, with rivals—especially FactSet (FDS)—cutting prices aggressively. While we believe LSEG has responded well and strengthened its offering from a lower price point, these pressures have created short-term headwinds. Additionally, the emergence of advanced AI models, such as Anthropic's Claude for Financial Services, has raised questions about potential disruption to financial information services. However, we view these large language models more as new distribution channels for LSEG's proprietary data than as threats to its core business. Despite our continued confidence in the company's long-term outlook over the next 24 months, these near-term challenges have led us to trim the position.

PROCEPT BioRobotics develops robotic systems for treating benign prostatic hyperplasia and is rapidly expanding in a largely underpenetrated market, supported by strong clinical evidence, improving insurance coverage and superior outcomes. Shares fell despite reporting 55% year-over-year revenue growth and rising margins, due to the unexpected retirement of its CEO. We view the appointment of new CEO Larry Wood, a former Edwards Lifesciences (EW) executive, as a meaningful upgrade to lead the company's next phase of growth.

Wingstop, a quick-service restaurant franchisor, reported quarterly results that exceeded expectations, but shares declined due to broader weakness across the restaurant industry. We remain confident in key growth initiatives, including expanded national advertising and the rollout of smart kitchen technology. Early results showed this smart kitchen technology has reduced customer service wait times by 40% within weeks of implementation, improving operational efficiency and enhancing the customer experience at minimal cost to franchisees. We are also optimistic about the planned 2026 launch of Wingstop's loyalty program, designed to enhance customer engagement and encourage repeat business. We added to our position during the quarter.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we initiated new positions in Teledyne Technologies (TDY) and Waystar (WAY). Teledyne provides advanced sensing, transmission and analysis technologies across niche markets, including digital imaging, instrumentation, and aerospace and defense electronics. Its aerospace and defense segment is benefiting from rising global military spending, which is driving demand for high-performance sensors, drones and space systems. Flagship products like nano drones and proprietary sensor technologies position Teledyne well to meet evolving defense needs. The company's industrial markets are also recovering, supported by trends in automation and testing. Teledyne has consistently generated strong free cash flow, and management has a proven track record of value creation through disciplined M&A. With accelerating defense demand and a rebound in industrial markets, we see potential for mid-teens EPS growth driven by both organic growth and strategic expansion.

Waystar is a leading cloud-based revenue cycle management platform that supports over 30,000 health care organizations, including 16 of the top 20 US hospitals. We initiated a position based on our view that Waystar is well positioned to benefit from the fallout of last year's cyberattack on its largest competitor, Change Healthcare. While provider switching takes time, early signs indicate this shift is underway. Additionally, we believe Waystar stands to gain from integrating AI capabilities into its software, delivering measurable ROI by automating denial management and prior authorizations, helping providers overcome staffing constraints and improve claims performance.

In addition to Wingstop, we also added to RBC Bearings (RBC) and Parsons (PSN) during the quarter. RBC Bearings is a leading provider of specialty bearings, gearing and motion control products, with over 70% of revenue derived from sole- or primary-sourced components—underscoring its critical role in customer supply chains. Its strategy of producing ahead of demand supports strong delivery performance and quality, while proprietary design software enhances product engineering. We added to our position during the quarter, as we believe the company is well positioned to benefit from the production ramp-up of next-generation aircraft, supported by long-term contracts with Boeing (BA) and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY), as well as steady aftermarket demand for maintenance and repair. The company recently issued a positive outlook, supported by a strong backlog and the successful integration of its VACCO acquisition, both of which signal continued growth potential.

Parsons is a technology-driven defense, intelligence and infrastructure engineering firm recognized as a leader in cybersecurity, missile defense and critical infrastructure protection for government and commercial clients. We believe the company is well positioned to capitalize on multiple secular growth drivers—including rising global defense spending, increased North American infrastructure investment fueled by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, expanding infrastructure development in the Middle East as economies diversify beyond oil, and long-term opportunities in per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances remediation. We believe Parsons remains early in its growth journey relative to more established peers and is poised to deliver high-single-digit to low-double-digit organic revenue growth over the next 3–5 years, with modest margin expansion supporting a mid-teens compound annual growth rate in earnings per share.

We ended our investment campaigns in Atlassian and Bright Horizons (BFAM) during the quarter. Atlassian is a leading provider of team collaboration software. We believe the company's recent results reinforce the rationale behind our decision to exit the position. While cloud revenue growth remains healthy at 26% year-over-year, the deceleration from prior quarters suggests limited near-term acceleration in cloud migration. Ongoing concerns about Atlassian's seat-based pricing model, alongside broader uncertainty about the impact of AI on knowledge-based jobs, continue to weigh on sentiment. In our view, Atlassian must evolve from its traditional SaaS model toward a more usage-based or value-based monetization approach. However, this strategic shift lacks strong valuation support unless executed rapidly and effectively. Our evolving perspective on SaaS fundamentals over the past six months, combined with the belief that meaningful upside is unlikely until its AI investments begin delivering substantial revenue, ultimately led us to fully exit the position.

Bright Horizons is a leading provider of employer-sponsored childcare and dependent care, offering full-service centers and flexible backup care well suited to hybrid work environments. We initiated our position in December 2024, anticipating a return-to-office catalyst. With over 60% of earnings from its scalable backup care business and underutilized centers expected to benefit from rising enrollments, we saw long-term potential. However, despite early signs of recovery, enrollment growth has remained modest and uneven, particularly in younger classrooms. Continued macro uncertainty and delayed office returns have created near-term visibility challenges, leading us to exit the position.

In addition to London Stock Exchange, notable trims in the quarter included Tyler Technologies and Clearwater Analytics (CWAN). Tyler Technologies provides end-to-end information management solutions and services for local government offices. When we purchased the stock in 2020, our investment thesis focused on its potential for durable growth, driven by the resilience of its end markets and the opportunity presented by its transition to cloud-based subscriptions. As anticipated, the shift to the cloud has accelerated revenue growth and contributed to margin expansion. We trimmed our position during the quarter as the stock's valuation increasingly reflected these benefits, but we continue to hold it in the Crop.

Clearwater Analytics provides cloud-based investment accounting, compliance and risk reporting solutions for insurers, corporations and asset managers. Recently, the company shifted its strategy from focusing on organic growth to pursuing larger scale M&A, deploying significant capital and taking on leverage. This pivot was not part of our original investment thesis, so we trimmed our position. Given the high quality of the franchise, we continue to monitor it closely for evidence that the company can successfully execute this new strategy.

data.title: WeShop: Avoid This Meme Stock
data.description:
Shares of WeShop (WSHP) have been an extraordinary performer since launching via a direct listing IPO last week. While we have seen many high-flying momentum stocks get hit hard during the recent bout of market turbulence, this performance is a reminder that small-float stocks can see dramatic interest upon generating “meme” interest (there has been considerable discussion of shares on X for example). Often though as we have seen in past situations, these situations can be short-lived. With a unique business model and significant retail interest, now is a good time to analyze WSHP’s prospects. I would steer clear of shares.

WeShop has no material earnings power yet

WeShop is a UK based shopping social network with plans to expand to the US. What sets it apart from virtually any other company is its “ShareBack” program. Essentially, every time you make a purchase with its app or refer a new member to open their account, you earn shares in the stock. This is a fairly unique mode that has no clear comparisons. With 23 million shares outstanding, the company has a $4.6 billion market capitalization, so there is considerable excitement about it.

AMC (AMC) is a company whose stock over the past few years has seen significant volatility amid “meme” crazes. It has leant into its following among retailer traders with its investor connect program, which is essentially gives AMC shareholders additional perks. This is an example of a company rewarding shareholders with business perks. WeShop essentially inverts this model, giving customers equity in its business.

Now, as you can see in its prospectus, Weshop is still a company in its infancy. It ended March with just 72,000 transacting customers, and over ~3 years, total volumes were below $150 million, even as it has now signed up 500 UK retail partners. After growing steadily during 2022-2023, user count has basically stopped as it focused on its expansion into the US.

In all of 2024, it generated 1.3 million pounds of sales with 12.2 million of costs. Gross margin was -4.3 million, and G&A of 4.1 million was more than 3x revenue. This business is still in its infancy, and investors buying shares of WSHP are really buying into the promise of what could be rather than any existing financial performance. In the first half of 2025, sales were below 300k, down by more than half from last year, and it lost 4 million. This decline was because it completed its pilot proof of concept work and pivoted focus towards its planned expansion into the US. As of June 30th, the company had ~$100k of cash and a book equity value of 4 million GBP. Again, its market cap is now ~$4 billion.

Its stock rewards program is unique

Now, the key to its business model is its equity program.Under its Shareback program, WeShop gives WePoints equal to 80% of its commission. On a $100 purchase, WeShop may earn $10. Of this, it will pay out $8 worth of WePoints. It calculates WePoints based on where shares are trading. So if the stock is at $4 the prior day, it will pay out 2 WePoints. In essence, each WePoint is worth 1 share. For every user you refer, you receive 5% of the WePoints they receive over the next year. If someone you referred earns 100 WePoints, WeShop would give you another 5 WePoints. This encourages existing WeShop users to bring others to the platform, a key way that WeShop aims to grow. The more users it brings on to its app, the more retail platforms it can sign up.

Users on the app can share reviews, etc., with their friends in an effort to create a shopping social network. Companies will sign up to WeShop’s affiliate program in an effort to sell product to the userbase and boost their own sales. In a sense, they are leveraging WeShop’s Shareback program as a loyalty program. In addition to affiliate revenue, WeShop aims to generate growing advertising revenue, putting featured products on its homepage, giving it higher placement, etc. This can help affiliates drive even more sales.

There are several things that need to occur for the company to be a success. First, it will need to bring on a sufficient number of retail affiliates to its platform. I actually view this as the easiest to achieve. It has had success in the UK bringing retailers on, including large ones like Shein and eBay (EBAY). Many retailers sell through third-party channels where they share some economics (think of the Amazon marketplace for instance), or through pooled loyalty programs like Capital One (COF) offers.

The next question is how many users it can subscribe and how much interest there is in the equity rewards program. Here, I am somewhat skeptical. There are many loyalty programs that offer cash back (think of credit cards), miles, or free items (ie buy 10 coffees, get the 11th free). Redeeming a loyalty credit of some kind is a fairly standard activity for American consumers. The currency of the loyalty program, WSHP equity, is the uniqueness of WeShop’s offering.

The question is how appealing receiving equity a loyalty will be vs something with discrete value like cash back. WeShop is leaning very much in its prospectus and marketing into the concept of communal ownership, and perhaps this will have appeal, though there is so much competition in the loyalty space that I question the viability of this program. Plus, its business is likely to be highly correlated to WSHP’s share price. When the stock is surging, accrued WePoints will become more valuable, which may increase traffic to its platform, but the reverse is also true.

Affiliate margins will be low; advertising is key

However, even if WeShop can build a larger user base, I question how profitable of a business it ever can be. By returning 80% of affiliate fees to customers, gross margins can be no more than 20% to start. Now, this is a non-cash expense, but its share count will rapidly rise. In practice, we would likely see it use its growing cash position to buy shares to offset the dilution from WePoints. Still, on every $10 it earns in affiliate fees, it pays $8 to customers. With ongoing costs to maintain the app, secure data, and pay staff, we are unlikely to see WSHP ever turn a profit on its affiliate fees. At best, this is a breakeven business given sub-20% gross margins.

A company like DoorDash (DASH) has a 47% gross margin and a 34% contribution margin, having achieved massive scale. With an 80-85% payout (including the 5% referral payment), gross margins are unlikely to be more than 15%. Assuming a similar 13% of below gross margin costs, its affiliate business will be basically breakeven, or slightly profitable at best.

Instead, this is functionally, as far as profitability is concerned, an advertising company. In essence, WeShop’s model is to break even on its affiliation fees to rewards shoppers enough that it can build a large marketplace that leads to material advertising spending by participating retailers. The question is how much ad revenue this platform can generate. Pinterest has $1 billion in quarterly revenue on 600 million users, or about $6-7 per user per year. Wal-Mart (WMT) has been able to pass $4 billion in ad revenue.

Reverse engineering, at ~20x earnings, WSHP would need to generate ~$200 million in net income to justify its share price. Assuming its affiliation business breaks even and its ad business can run at 40% after-tax margins, WSHP would need to do $500 million of ad revenue a year to justify where shares trade. That would be reaching a tenth of Wal-Mart’s ad revenue in 2025. Even at $60/user in revenue (10x Pinterest’s economics), that would require reaching 8 million monthly active users.

Too much optimism is priced in

My point is that WeShop’s share price is already discounting an extraordinary amount of growth. This may turn into a sizable business, but it has to become one to justify where shares are trading. Plus, its model does not appear differentiated. There is nothing stopping Delta (DAL), for instance, from offering customers to redeem SkyMiles in DAL stock vs free Delta flights. Virtually any public company can do that. I am not sure how popular the WePoints concept will be vs traditional loyalty offerings.

I am skeptical that this business ever achieves scale, but even if I am wrong, the stock is discounting WSHP accumulating several million users and becoming a major advertising business. With so much good news priced in and this company not having any material presence yet in the US, I expect WSHP to be like so many meme stocks we have seen. While the rally is incredibly powerful, shares will likely push lower over time. With such extreme volatility, I would never outright short WSHP, as it could go higher first. That said, in time, fundamentals will win out, and I am highly skeptical this will be a multi-billion dollar business. I view shares as a strong sell as the business is unlikely to ever grow into the valuation already being ascribed to it.

data.title: Euro Stabilizes Above $1.15, While The Yen Remains Vulnerable
data.description:
Overview

The US dollar (DXY) is little changed against most of the G10 currencies as the North American session is about to get underway. There were some follow-through greenback gains after yesterday's advance but limited. The dollar's rally against the yen continued. It reached almost JPY157.80 before pulling back to around JPY157.10, where it found support in Europe. Yesterday's FOMC minutes seemed to raise the hurdle to a rate cut next month, and this coupled with Nvidia's earnings and guidance helped set today's tone.

Equities soared in the Asia-Pacific region. Taiwan's (TWSE) market led the way with a nearly 3.2% gain and the Nikkei (NKY:IND) rallied 2.6%. Only Chinese stocks, both on the mainland (SHSZ300) and in Hong Kong (HSI), fell. Europe's STOXX 600 (STOXX) is snapping a five-day drop and is up almost 0.80% in late European morning turnover. US index futures (SPX) point to a gap higher opening on Wall Street today. A large fiscal package by Japan continues to weigh on JGB yields. The 10-year yield rose five basis points today. European yields are slightly firmer, except for Gilts, which are virtually flat. The US 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is little changed around 4.13-4.14%. Gold is softer, near $4065, while January WTI is consolidating below $60, which is where the 20-day moving average is found.

USD: The Dollar Index rose by about 0.65% yesterday, its largest increase since late September. More notable, it settled above the 200-day moving average for the first time since early March. It edged a little higher today but did not quite make it, though, to the month's high, which was near 100.35. Beyond that, the next important technical target is around 101.55, the (38.2%) retracement of this year's decline. Belatedly, the September jobs report will be released today. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey projects a 55k increase (22k in August). Private sector payrolls are seen rising by 68k (38k in August), even though the ADP estimate was for a loss of 29k. The unemployment rate is expected to be steady at 4.3%. Governor Waller's argument for another rate cut next month is that the labor market has stalled, while the weakness has not shown fully in the data, and he wants to look through the tariff increase in some goods prices. Employment data is generally regarded as a lagging indicator, and even more so now with the September figures. Therefore, barring a significant surprise, it is not clear that the data will change many minds either in the markets or at the central bank.

Euro: The euro slid to slightly below $1.1520 yesterday, a nine-session low, and in the process took out the (61.8%) retracement of the leg up from earlier this month. Follow-through selling today took it to $1.1510. The $1.1500 level is key now. There are 2 bln euros of options expiring there today and another 1.4 bln euro option that expires tomorrow. The price action weakened the technical condition, and the momentum indicators are threatening to turn lower. The five-day moving average, which had crossed above the 20-day moving average last week for the first time since late September, is now poised to move back below it. The low earlier this month was about $1.1470. The previous low was in late July/early August, slightly below $1.14, which is where the 200-day moving average is now found. Ahead of tomorrow's preliminary November PMI, the eurozone reported September construction output. It fell by 0.5%, but Q3 was flat - making it the first quarter this year it has not fallen. Still, with Q3 GDP in hand, the impact on markets and the central bank is minimal.

CNY: Consistent with its broad gains, the dollar rose for the fourth consecutive session against the offshore yuan. It briefly poked above CNH7.12 and settled above the 20-day moving average (~CNH7.1160). The CNH7.12 area, which was briefly frayed today, corresponded with the (61.8%) retracement of this month's dollar decline. The next level of chart resistance is seen around CNH7.1275. The firmer greenback saw the PBOC raise the dollar's reference rate (CNY7.0905 vs. CNY7.0872 yesterday). It was lifted for the third consecutive session, matching the longest streak since April. As expected, China's loan prime rates were held steady at 3.0% for the one-year tenor and 3.5% for the five-year loan. The rates were shaved by 10 bp once this year in May, after being cut twice for a total of 35 bp last year. Critics of China want it to stimulate domestic demand and significantly strengthen the yuan simultaneously. And it is not simply to stimulate domestic demand that is being demanded, but that it buys more of the world's goods - despite large parts of the world, including the US, Europe, and Japan, applying export controls to the PRC.

JPY: The dollar rose to around JPY157.20 in North America yesterday. It extended the gains seen in Europe after a quiet drift lower in the local session. It reached JPY157.80 today before steadying. The high for the year was recorded on January 10 near JPY158.90. The 2024 high was closer to JPY162. For the first time in a little more than a month, the dollar settled well above the upper Bollinger Band (found ~JPY156 today). The yen's weakness is beginning to impact expectations for the Bank of Japan. After eight consecutive days of falling, the swaps market increased the odds slightly of a BOJ hike next month from slightly below 30% to slightly more than 33%, but it has fallen to a new low near 20% today, despite comments from a BOJ official who seemed to suggest a December hike should not be ruled out. The US 10-year premium over Japan was about 358 bp in mid-January, the year's high, and late last month, it recorded the year's low near 230 bp. It is now near 232 bp. It had not been lower since April 2022. Japan's 30-year bond yield will post its sixth consecutive rise this year. It passed the 1% yield mark in 2022, the 2% yield mark in 2024 and the 3% yield mark this year. Nearly every threshold that was crossed brought out pundits claiming that a threshold was crossed and Japanese investors would stop exporting their savings. Through last week, Japanese investors bought more foreign bonds this year than last year's (JPY268 bln) weekly average vs. an average of JPY117 bln in the same year-ago period). Japanese investors also bought foreign equities this year (JPY171.5 bln weekly average) after being net sellers in the same period last year and in 2023. In summary, the weekly MOF data shows Japanese investors exporting a weekly average of about JPY440 bln of their savings this year after buying an average of almost JPY67 bln a week of foreign bonds and stocks in the same 2024 period.

GBP: Sterling peaked last week near $1.3215 and has not looked back. It has recorded lower highs since last Thursday's high, but yesterday the floor gave way. It held above last Thursday's low near $1.31, but was driven through yesterday to reach almost $1.3050. A marginal new low was recorded today slightly below $1.3040, before recovering to new session highs near $1.3085. The month's low was set November 4-5, near $1.3010. Recall that the (50%) retracement of this year's rally is about $1.2945. The UK economic calendar is between yesterday's CPI and tomorrow's retail sales and preliminary November PMI. The swaps market is discounting almost an 88% chance of a BOE rate cut next month, up around 2/3rd at the end of last month. A cut would bring the base rate to 3.75%. The swaps market sees scope for one more cut in the cycle and about a 40% chance of another before the end of next year.

CAD: Even with a 0.4% loss yesterday, the Canadian dollar was among the strongest G10 currencies. The greenback still made a marginal new eight-day high yesterday near CAD1.4065, which is holding so far today. The CAD1.4075 area corresponds to the (61.8%) retracement of the US dollar pullback from CAD1.4140, the seven-month high recorded earlier this month. Recall that the halfway mark of this year's range is about CAD1.4165. Canada reports October industrial product and raw material prices today. Neither captures the attention of the market. October CPI was reported on Monday, and the 0.2% month-over-month rise translated into a 2.2% year-over-year pace. The swaps market not only thinks that the Bank of Canada's easing cycle ended last month, but almost a 25% chance of a hike in Q4 '26 is being discounted.

AUD: The Australian dollar recorded yesterday's low after European markets closed near $0.6450, its lowest level since October 17, and took out the 200-day (~$0.6460) moving average on an intraday basis for the first time here in H2 '25. It is trading in a narrow range today of roughly $0.6470-0.6490. There are options for A$735 mln at $0.6500 that expire today. The October lows were in the $0.6540-45 area. A break could risk a move toward $0.6400, which is roughly the (38.2%) retracement of this year's rally and ahead of which the Aussie found support in July and August. The highs for the year were recorded on September 17 (Fed Day) slightly above $0.6705. Since then, it has had three multi-day rallies that stalled at progressively lower levels. The momentum indicators are turning lower, and the five-day moving average is back below the 20-day moving average.

MXN: The dollar peaked on Tuesday near MXN18.4920 and reversed to finish lower on the day. Follow-through selling yesterday took it to around MXN18.3050 before recovering, and the dollar settled above MXN18.36. It has been mostly confined to an MXN18.33-18.37 range so far today. Recall that last Friday and Monday's lows were in the MXN18.2980-18.3015 area. About $520 mln options at MXN18.30 expire today. A break could spur a move to last week's low (~MXN18.2530) or this year's low recorded on September 17 (Fed Day) at MXN18.20.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fed's Mixed Signals From Minutes Raise Doubts About Rate Cuts
data.description:
Earlier this week we asked: Is Wall Street starting to second-guess the inflation outlook? The Fed’s adding to the suspense — minutes from the Oct. 28–29 policy meeting reveal growing doubts about the case for more rate cuts.

"In discussing the near-term course of monetary policy, participants expressed strongly differing views about what policy decision would most likely be appropriate at the committee's December meeting," the minutes said.

Fed funds futures reacted quickly as traders dialed down expectations for another round of policy easing at the Dec. 10 FOMC meeting. Earlier in the week, the market was pricing in a coin flip on next month’s rate decision. Following yesterday’s release of Fed minutes, the estimated probability is now firmly favoring no cut (68%).

It’s best to take all this with a grain of salt. The government shutdown delayed several economic reports that will be appearing ahead of the next Fed meeting, starting with today’s delayed payrolls report for September. But there are also holes that won’t be filled, keeping the guesswork front and center. The US Labor Department canceled the October payrolls report, citing the government shutdown.

The unusual degree of fog on profiling the economy is lifting, but it’s reasonable to wonder if today’s assumptions could shift by more than a trivial degree between now and Dec. 10. The hawks and doves at the Fed are still debating if a third rate cut is appropriate next month. Presumably the case will strengthen, one way or another, during the next three weeks.

“I’m not decided going into the December meeting,” Chicago Federal Reserve president Austan Goolsbee told Yahoo Finance in an interview. “I am nervous about the inflation side of the ledger, where you’ve seen inflation above the target for 4.5 years and it’s trending the wrong way.”

One thing that hasn’t changed is the implied forecast for rate cuts via the policy-sensitive US 2-year Treasury yield. This key rate ticked up yesterday to 3.60%, but continues to trade in a range that’s moderately below the Fed’s current 3.75%-4.0% target. A sustained rise from current levels, however, would be a clear sign that the bond market sentiment is shifting toward the hawks.

Betting markets, however, are convinced that the Fed will leave rates unchanged next month: Polymarket is currently pricing in 76% odds for no change.

With economic data still trickling in and Fed sentiment split, December’s rate decision remains a moving target. Futures and betting markets are favoring a no-cut scenario next month, but the current confidence may be more fragile than it looks.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Maple Gold Mines: A Small Story With A Big Partner - Agnico Eagle's Quiet Bet
data.description:
There are projects that tend to call our attention not because of their current magnitude, but because of the type of structure that keeps them going, and when I analyze Maple Gold Mines (OTCQB:MGMLF), I have no doubts that we are looking at one of those cases.

In a market where we are used to seeing junior miners moving at the rhythm of their financing rounds, Maple stands out from the rest because it doesn't exclusively rely on the market to exist. Behind MGMLF, we see that there is a world-scale associate like Agnico Eagle (AEM), which not only contributes credibility, resources and technical knowledge, but I also consider it to have a validation role that very few explorers have at this point of the story.

Though the story of Maple may no longer be new, what still remains so is its strategic proposal. The Douay and Joutel projects are found right at the heart of the Abitibi Greenstone Belt, one of the world's most prolific auriferous provinces. AEM knew that zone long before partnering, given that was precisely where the Canadian company operated its first mine between 1974 and 1993, and from my point of view, that geological and technical continuity grants the project a value that transcends numbers.

Upon looking further into the "hardest" numbers, Maple nowadays has control over 481 km² of ground and possesses more than 3.0 MM oz of gold in certified resources. The goal, according to their own plan, is to reach 4.0 MM oz in 2026, a number that would place it in the league of Abitibi's mid-tier developers. And a point that I consider important to highlight is that this growth doesn't rely on promises, but on an already financed exploration program, a reinforced team and an associate that actively takes part in the technical direction.

On the other hand, the business closed a LIFE private placement on October 23, 2025 for C$13MM, which increased its total cash to C$ 20.5 MM, covering the exploration budget through 2026. In the words of its CEO, Kiran Patankar:

We are very pleased to have closed this upsized and oversubscribed financing, which bolsters our balance sheet and fully funds key catalysts through 2026 … Strong support from leading institutional investors, including one of the world's largest precious metals funds taking a 9.5 % stake, speaks both to the quality of our Québec assets and the market's confidence in our team and growth strategy.

In my opinion, that back-up gives it air and time, two factors that tend to dwindle in the world of juniors, while at the same time allowing it to sustain a less speculative strategy and one of more value construction.

According to the Investor Deck from November 2025, MGMLF is trading at an EV/oz Au Eq. multiple of $16, against an average near $42 among its peers from Abitibi, which I consider to be a gap difficult to justify if we were to consider the scale of the project and the institutional profile of its shareholding.

For all this, I see Maple as a story of speculative growth, but backed by true fundamentals, based on district-scale geology, a solid strategic alliance and a clear roadmap. If the team manages to accomplish the proposed feats in its last presentation, such as the update of resources and preliminary study in 2026, from my point of view, the market should acknowledge it.

This way, my posture is (Speculative) Buy, with a horizon of 12 - 24 months and a revaluation potential nearing 70-80 %, aligned with a partial normalization of its EV/oz multiple (from a current $16 towards $ 28-30), if the market begins to acknowledge its relative value.

A district with history and scale

The belt of Abitibi is a name familiar to any geologist or mining investor. It's a corridor with more than 180 MM oz of produced gold, consolidated infrastructure and an industrial ecosystem that drastically reduces development costs. And in this scenario, Maple occupies a privileged place among that frame, where it operates over the Douay-Joutel trend, a structural sequence that has been historically prolific.

According to the Investor Deck from November 2025, it is contemplated that the company has control over 100% of an auriferous district of approximately 481 km², with an established mineral resource of ~3.0 MM oz in Douay, a high - grade mining complex previously in production in Joutel, and a significant exploration potential throughout the whole corridor.

The same material highlights that the Project is strategically placed in Abitibi's auriferous belt, a region with a wide mining history and an exceptional infrastructure, factors that contribute to the development plan's operative solidity.

The Douay block contributes the foundation of current resources, which comprise more than 3.0 MM oz according to SLR Consulting 2022, while Joutel represents the organic growth opportunity. For this second block, Maple seeks to publish its first resource calculation in 2026, revisiting historical high-grade zones like Eagle West and Telbel.

A point that I consider important to highlight is that both projects are connected by road and fed by the Hydro-Québec electric network, which constitutes a logistical advantage that reduces the initial capital requirements and improves the projected returns.

A clear and financed roadmap

One of the most solid aspects of the Maple case, from my point of view, is its planning. With the financing obtained in October this year, which I have already previously mentioned, the business executes a program of 30,000 metres of drilling that covers both the expansion in Douay (Nika and 531) and the resource definition in Joutel (Eagle-Telbel).

The corporate presentation specifies it very clearly:

Phase II Douay drilling (~20,000 m planned this Fall/Winter)… Maiden Joutel drill program (~10,000 m planned this Fall/Winter). Remaining Budget to 2026 YE-C$ 13.7 M (Largest in MGM History).

This phrase sums up the difference between a promise and a plan, given that Maple doesn't rely on a financial "yes" to drill, but already has the funds, the contracts and the schedule.

The proposed goal in the last Investor Deck is carrying out a new MRE (resources update) and a scoping study (preliminary engineering study) in the H1 2026, both necessary steps to evaluate a future PEA or prefeasibility.

In the meantime, the program's total budget (C$ 13.7 MM) and the distribution of perforated metres are found detailed right up ahead:

The value of the partner

Sometimes, the best indicator of an explorer's potential doesn't rely on its geology, but on who bets on it, and in the case of Maple, that name is Agnico Eagle. The Canadian miner not only possesses nearly 13% of the basic capital, but it also maintains a back-in stock right that will allow it to recover up to 50% of the Douay-Joutel project if a minimum NPV5% of C$300MM were to be reached.

The description of this strategic partnership on behalf of Maple is clear:

Largest shareholder with ~13.1% basic ownership (~14% partially-diluted) post October 2025 raise. (…).

Maintains a strategic interest in Douay/Joutel via a 1% Dilution NSR and a back-in right to acquire a 50% interest in Douay/Joutel by paying C$12M + 2× cumulative expenditures until Maple Gold achieves key development milestone.

Afterwards, in that same presentation, AEM's operative role is also detailed:

Continued involvement through financings and technical collaboration provides 3rd-party validation of geology and enhances technical rigor.

This way, it remains clear that this alliance is not symbolic, but Agnico contributes quality control in geological models, QA/QC review and a strategic guide over drilling. I have no doubts that participation of a producer with active operations in the same province reduces that technical risk and increases Maple's future development possibilities.

Financial position, Q3 results and cash horizon

To get a more complete picture, although Maple remains an exploratory company with no revenue, I consider it important to stop at its recent financial situation. According to the financial statements of Q3, MGMLF had exploration and evaluation expenses close to C$1.18M, a level that I consider consistent with the pace of drilling executed at Douay and Joutel. General and administrative expenses were around C$0.83M, a figure that, in my view, remains within a reasonable range for a team coordinating several technical campaigns at the same time.

With this cost structure, the net loss for the period was close to C$1.63M. It is a result that fits what we normally see in a junior actively investing in its exploration program.

If we look at the 9M 2025, the net cash used in operating activities shows an accumulated output close to C$5.06M. That number gives a clearer sense of how the project is actually consuming cash. As of September 30, Maple had C$7.54M in cash, an amount that alone gave it three to four quarters of autonomy at the current pace of spending.

However, the situation shifted after the LIFE funding in October, which raised available resources to approximately C$20.5M. From my perspective, this reinforcement gives Maple air and time, which in the junior world is a strategic advantage. It lets the company execute its roadmap without depending on additional rounds in the short term, and keep moving forward with a program already funded from start to finish.

Valuation and re-rating potential

Now turning to analyzing the market, we see that Maple still trades as an unnamed junior, while in reality it already has all the elements of a future mid-tier developer.

According to last deck's stats, the company's EV/oz multiple is $16 per Au Eq. ounce, against an average of $42 among its peers, Troilus, Probe, Banyan, Wallbridge and others, such as we see in the following graphic.

After the previous analysis, I consider that the current difference is explained not by geological risk, but by the visibility of the market. When the new resource estimation and the engineering study are published in 2026, the gap should narrow, and I would even go as far as estimating that the partial convergence toward the peers' multiples could imply a revaluation above 70%, such as I mentioned before.

Stock market liquidity and traded volume

Another aspect that I consider relevant, especially since it is a microcap, is the daily liquidity of the asset. In the case of MGMLF, the ticker traded in the US, the average volume is around 70 000 shares/day. While I do not consider it to be a high standard, it is consistent with an early-stage exploration company and a shareholding base that mixes retail investors with growing institutional participation.

In practice, this means that trades usually run smoothly for moderate amounts. Larger orders may require a bit more patience, which is typical for this segment. Liquidity tends to improve as the company reaches visible milestones such as resource updates or the release of the preliminary study.

In my view, the combination of a funded program and a strategic partner of the calibre of Agnico Eagle gives Maple future visibility, which should eventually help to strengthen the volume dynamic as well.

Conclusion

Having reached the end of the study, I have no more doubts that what interests me most is not only its numerical potential, but the way it manages its expectations. In a sector where the juniors are sold as short - term stories, I see that Maple advances in a sustained manner, with no hurry but relentlessly, counting on two key factors, such as the financing and the institutional validation. Besides, its team has experience in projects similar to Abitibi, its cost structure is austere and its technical communication transmits more realism that enthusiasm.

The risks are those habitual of an explorer, such as the resource conversion, grade variability and gold's price sensitivity. However, a point that I highlight is that Maple doesn't promise discoveries, but focuses on the construction of an economically viable district, something that often makes the difference in mining.

When the junior company combines capital, discipline and an associate the size and reputation of Agnico Eagle, it ceases to be just an exploratory gamble, and it turns into a foundational piece within the map of Abitibi's great players. That is a position that the market still doesn't fully acknowledge, but that, to my criteria, has a much higher value than the one its current quoting reflects. That's the reason I consider that MGMLF is a Buy opportunity, a story of growth within the most competitive segment of Canadian mining.

data.title: Diamond Hill Core Bond Strategy Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index returned 2.03% during Q3 of 2025, bringing year-to-date performance for the Index to 6.13%. 2025 year-to-date performance represents the best nine-month start to a calendar year since 2020’s 6.79%. Q3 brought a mix of market-moving developments: the long-delayed tariffs were implemented on August 7; the labor market showed signs of weakness and a dovish shift from the Federal Reserve at Jackson Hole coincided with growing concerns over a potential government shutdown. As in Q2, risk assets have continued to shrug off economic and political headwinds, with investor demand keeping spreads near historically tight levels. Overall, Q3 reflected a market increasingly focused on central bank policy, with credit markets showing resilience despite mounting macroeconomic uncertainty.

Shifts at the Federal Reserve

After an unexplained absence at the July 30 and 31 meeting of the FOMC, former Federal Reserve Governor Adriana Kugler submitted her resignation from the board on August 1. Kugler was nominated and confirmed to the Fed board in 2023, and she was set to serve the remainder of Lael Brainard’s 14-year term, expiring in January 2026. While no explanation was offered from Kugler regarding the reasoning for her departure just months ahead of her term expiring, the vacancy paved the way for President Trump to fill another position on the influential board. Stephen Miran, who is currently the chair of Trump’s Council of Economic Advisors (CEA), was nominated for the position and confirmed in the days leading up to the September FOMC meeting. Miran distinguished himself at his first meeting by being the only member to advocate for a rate cut of 50 basis points (bps).

Kugler’s departure four months before the expiration of her term was a bit of a surprise, as she offered no forewarning. An even bigger surprise was the President’s attempt to terminate Lisa Cook, who was nominated by President Joe Biden in 2022 and confirmed in 2023 to serve a 14-year term as a member of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors. President Trump announced his intention to fire Governor Cook on August 25, citing allegations that she committed mortgage fraud. Bill Pulte, the director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, alleges that Cook claimed two primary residences in 2021 to obtain better terms on both mortgages, as mortgage rates on second homes and investment properties are often higher than those for primary residences. The Federal Reserve Act states that governors can only be removed “for cause,” meaning malfeasance, neglect of duty or inefficiency. Establishing a for-cause removal typically requires a proceeding that would allow Cook, according to legal experts, to answer the charges and present evidence, which hasn’t happened in this case.

On September 9, a federal judge blocked Cook’s removal from the Federal Reserve Board, allowing her lawsuit to proceed and for her to remain as Fed governor until her lawsuit is heard, and she was able to attend the September FOMC meeting. The case now moves before the Supreme Court, which will decide whether the President was within his power to terminate a member of the Federal Reserve. The outcome of this case will have far-reaching implications — not just for the Federal Reserve’s leadership, but for the foundational belief in its independence from political influence.

The Next Easing Cycle Begins

Despite the shifts in the composition of the Federal Reserve and the ongoing uncertainty around Lisa Cook’s term, the FOMC moved forward with the first adjustment to Fed Funds since December 2024. The decision to ease reflected a “shift in the balance of risks,” with the Committee believing that the “downside risks to employment have risen.” Inflation has ticked up since the spring — a trend acknowledged in the post-meeting statement. At the same time, job growth has slowed sharply, and the unemployment rate has reached the upper end of the Committee’s central range consistent with full employment.

Powell’s post-meeting press conference was mildly hawkish and signaled that there was no rush to get rates back to neutral. Powell characterized the adjustment as a “risk management cut” and expectations are for a “meeting-by-meeting” approach. The Committee’s ongoing bias to ease was more apparent in the September Summary of Economic Projections (SEP), with the median projection for the Fed Funds rate at year end slipping to 3.625% from 3.875% in the June SEP, implying most officials see an additional 50 bps of easing over the Committee’s two remaining meetings of 2025. The median estimation for 2026 signaled just one cut, but this masks a decent amount of dispersion across the participants. Just two of the 19 participants were at the median (3.375%), and the median was just one dot away from slipping another 25 bps to 3.125%.

Notably, the median estimate for the longer-run, “neutral” Fed Funds rate was unchanged at 3%. This underscores how most Committee members still view the policy setting as at least somewhat restrictive, giving some scope to lower the Fed Funds rate to cushion the labor market without outright accommodative monetary policy stoking higher inflation.

Looking back, there are similarities between this year’s actions and those taken in 2024. In September 2024, the Fed initiated easing with a 50 bps cut, compared to the 25 bps reduction in September 2025. However, the FOMC emerged from that meeting with what appears to be a replication of the actions taken in November and December 2024 — a reduction of 25 bps at both meetings. This year is slightly different as the next meeting is in October, but for now, the future path of rates based on the Fed SEP is another 50 bps lower over the next two meetings. Exhibit 1 illustrates the shift in expectations from the various members of the Federal Reserve for the remainder of 2025 as well as 2026, 2027 and longer-term. This shift, 25 bps lower across the near- and medium-term forecasts, reinforces the Committee’s cautious approach to easing while maintaining a steady long-run view of neutral policy at 3%.

NFP Revisions, Labor Market Weakening and an Inflation Update

Recent reports of a resilient labor market may have overstated the underlying strength. According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics’ preliminary benchmark revision to March 2025 non-farm payrolls, total employment was 911,000 lower than previously reported. This revision implies an average monthly job loss of 76,000 — a sharp contrast to previous estimates of growth. Collectively, the data suggests the recent slowdown follows a longer period of modest expansion, potentially justifying a more aggressive Fed easing cycle. It’s worth noting that this is a preliminary estimate; the final benchmark

revision will be published in February 2026. In 2024, the benchmark revision to March reporting was a reduction of 818,000 jobs, the record until this most recent report. Notably, 2024’s 818,000 job loss estimate was later revised to a loss of 598,000 jobs.

]In addition to the benchmark revision, broader labor market indicators paint a picture of sustained weakness. The average monthly job gains in 2025 have been 102,000. In comparison, job gains averaged 175,000 in 2024 and 209,000 in 2023.

Historic revisions to the non-farm payroll reports in May and June (reduction of 258,000 jobs created) led to the termination of the head of the Bureau of Labor Statistics, creating more uncertainty for markets regarding data validity. Despite this slowdown, unemployment remains well below the “natural level of unemployment”, averaging roughly 4.16% in 2024, though it should be noted that the past three months have seen an increase from 4.1% in June to 4.3% in August.

The Federal Reserve’s preferred measure of inflation, the core personal consumption expenditures (PCE), increased 2.9% compared to August 2024, in line with both market expectations and July’s reading. This marked the highest reading since February 2025 and was the fifth consecutive increase since April (2.6%). Rounding in the reported number shows July and August in line with one another at 2.9%, but digging into the details, the report indicates a month-over-month increase from 2.853% to 2.905%. Thus, the economic conundrum continues to plague the Federal Reserve — higher inflation requires higher rates, but a weaker labor market requires lower rates, and if both exist concurrently, which is the best area to attack? Given these dual pressures, the Fed appears poised to prioritize stabilizing the labor market while tolerating modestly elevated inflation — at least in the short term.

Treasury Update

The Federal Reserve has begun its latest easing cycle, but its duration will hinge on upcoming economic data. Market expectations for the future path of rates started in the latter days of the volatility brought on by Liberation Day, as the uncertainty around the breadth and depth of the tariffs fueled expectations for a more accommodating Fed. While the Fed held the line on rates during the summer, the weakening of the labor market outweighed the potential impact of stubborn inflation, and the September FOMC meeting brought the first 25 bps move since late 2024. The Treasury yield curve had been in a bull steepening shift leading into Q3, with a more stabilized spread between shorter and longer yields over the past three months. A bull steepening of the curve occurs when short-term rates move lower (fueled by an accommodative Federal Reserve), but market concern over longer-term inflation and increased Treasury issuance pushes long-term rates higher. Since the beginning of 2025, the spread between the yield on the 2-year and 10-year Treasury has grown from 0.33% to 0.54% while the spread between the 2-year and 30-year has jumped from 0.54% to 1.12% as of September.

While the Treasury market delivered positive performance during Q3 (+1.51% for the Bloomberg US Treasury Index), it lagged both the corporate and securitized sectors and pulled the overall performance of the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index lower. Returns were positive across the Treasury curve in Q3, fueled by rates grinding lower. The bull steepening curve shift we’ve seen throughout 2025 continued, albeit at a slower pace than prior quarters. The 2-year Treasury yield dropped from 3.72% to 3.61% while the 30-year Treasury yield slid from 4.77% to 4.73% as concerns around the fiscal situation and the impact of political impasse in Washington continued to grow.

Corporate Update

The investment grade corporate market, as tracked by the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index, posted its strongest quarterly return (+2.60%) since Q3 2024 (+5.84%), driven by spread tightening and strong investor demand. Except for the market disruption in Q2 around the announcement of tariffs, investment grade corporate spreads have continued to grind tighter throughout the year. Following that disruption, investor risk appetite returned, pushing spreads lower. Investment grade corporate spread levels moved almost 10 bps lower during Q3, rallying from 83.2 bps to 73.81 bps. Spreads in this sector reached their lowest level since May 1998 in the latter part of the quarter (71.7 bps).

The yield on the Corporate Index followed a similar path, dropping roughly 18 bps from the beginning of Q3 (4.99%) to the low point in mid-September (4.70%) before climbing slightly to finish at 4.81% on September 30. Investors continue to look for opportunities in the investment grade corporate space while issuers flock to meet demand. Nearly $400 billion in new issuance during the quarter brings year-to-date issuance to $1.3 trillion, 3% ahead of 2024’s year’s pace.

Securitized Update

The securitized market (as measured by the Bloomberg US Securitized: MBS, ABS, CMBS Index) delivered another strong quarter of performance, returning 2.38%. The sector has consistently delivered strong quarter-over-quarter performance since the start of the year, delivering the best nine-month performance to start a calendar year (+6.70%) since 2009’s 6.93%. Plain vanilla passthrough mortgage-backed securities (MBS), as measured by the Bloomberg US MBS Index, led the way,

generating 2.43%, followed closely by collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs), which returned 2.30% as measured by the ICE BofA CMO Index. Elsewhere within the securitized market, non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) continued to deliver decent performance (+1.87%) while shorter-duration asset-backed securities (ABS) (+1.64%) continued their slow and steady performance, benefiting from attractive spread levels and shorter duration. Like the investment grade corporate markets, spreads moved lower throughout Q3 across all segments of the securitized market.

While spreads have moved lower throughout risk assets, higher yielding and better structured securitized segments of the market continue to offer better relative value for managers willing to dig into the details on the structures and underlying collateral. Issuance in the securitized market remains strong, with both CMBS and non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) outpacing issuance over the comparable 2024 period and ABS only slightly behind. After falling off the pace during Q2, issuance across securitized sectors is within striking distance of the record levels reached in 2024.

While overall market performance was strong, the quarter was not without headline risk. Subprime auto ABS issuer Tricolor filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy following news that several banks disclosed potential losses tied to warehouse credit lines utilized by Tricolor, and the US Justice Department is conducting an ongoing investigation in alleged irregularities. Some subprime auto ABS deals from smaller issuers experienced slight spread widening in the immediate aftermath, but the reaction in the overall market was muted. The overall tone in the securitized market remains constructive heading into Q4.

Portfolio Positioning and Performance

The Core Bond portfolio performed nearly in line with the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index during Q3. Year to date, the strategy remained slightly ahead of the benchmark.

The duration of the portfolio moved slightly lower compared with this time last year, from roughly 96% to 94% of the benchmark, despite the gyrations felt in the Treasury market. Uncertainty surrounding the future path of rates, the impact of tariffs on the global economy and geopolitical events has kept the portfolio closer to the benchmark’s duration than in recent years, but still slightly below neutral.

From a relative standpoint, the portfolio’s Treasury allocation was the best performing sector, outpacing allocations to both the securitized and corporate sectors. The longer duration posture in Treasuries for the portfolio helped drive absolute performance, as the 30-year Treasury rallied significantly from 4.927% on September 1 to 4.731% to end the quarter, outpacing the benchmark allocation. However, the portfolio’s lower weighting in Treasuries slightly detracted from relative performance. The corporate allocation detracted from relative performance during the quarter due to a combination of trailing performance and an underweight compared to the benchmark. The securitized sector contributed the most to relative performance, fueled by strong performance from non-agency RMBS and non-agency CMBS, especially relative to comparable benchmark allocations. The agency RMBS allocation contributed marginally to relative performance while the portfolio’s passthrough RMBS allocation trailed that of the benchmark.

The portfolio continues to search for opportunities in the marketplace while maintaining a conservative risk profile relative to the index.

Bonds rated AAA, AA, A and BBB are considered investment grade.
