data.title: Exxon Mobil: Why Stability Trumps Higher Dividend Yield
data.description:
When I last covered Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) on October 21st, 2024 (with a “strong buy” rating) in my article "Exxon Mobil: Buy Before Earnings”, the stock was really beginning to flatline after a strong period of late pandemic-era gains. Back then, I was viewing this activity as a period of sideways consolidation that would likely resolve itself in the upward direction. However, for the most part, this did not turn out to be the case. While we have not seen any significant rallies over the last six months, major declines have still been absent and this is obviously a stock with an elevated dividend that has historically rewarded investors for being patient. In this analysis, I will be downgrading XOM to a “buy” rating and explaining some of the concerns that I have for this ticker in addition to defining some important price levels that might be used as an opportunity to buy the stock on dips into 2026.

In many ways, Exxon’s third quarter earnings report was actually strengthened by faith in the stock’s underlying fundamentals. Adjusted EPS of $1.88 beat consensus estimates by a healthy margin, and the production business generated $5.68 billion in earnings (which is a record figure for the company). These numbers were driven in large part by production in Guyana (which surpassed 700,000 bpd) and the Permian Basin (which came in close to 1,700,000 bpd). Both of these results were also records, and I think that the Guyana production numbers are particularly significant given the level of profitability that is typically generated by those assets. As a result, the per-barrel profitability that is currently seen at Exxon has seen a doubling over the last six years and I think this makes it clear that CEO Darren Woods has done an excellent job with cost-cutting during that period.

Now, this is not to say that the news is all positive. Net income has declined by -12% on an annualized basis (at $7.55 billion) and Exxon’s top-line revenue figure ($85.29 billion) fell -5% on the same timeline. But operating cash flow is now seen at roughly $14.8 billion and this is important because it lays the foundation for the share buybacks, the elevated dividend, and capex that is needed to continue transforming the business. With a run rate like this, Exxon should have enough firepower to support shareholder payout rewards and to continue enabling growth investments without seeing significant pressure on the balance sheet. Free cash flow of roughly $6.3 billion suggests that Exxon will have plenty of cash (after capex) to maintain dividend coverage even if we see oil prices fall further from here. In the chart above, we can see that BP p.l.c. (BP) does screen as being quite a bit cheaper from the price to free cash flow perspective (21.83x at Exxon versus a little over 9x at BP). But I think that a lot of that risk premium is driven by volatility in BP’s cash and earnings metrics and that Exxon still offers the most favorable balance of consistency in earnings and dividend coverage (when compared to the rest of this group).

Now, I’ll offer some potential negatives for the bull thesis as well. Primarily, it should be said that Exxon’s forward price-to-earnings valuation is not exactly a “steal” right now. A forward PE of 17.2x might be thought of as fair for Exxon, but BP is still cheaper (at 12.4x) and offers a bigger dividend (5.36%). What this tells me is that Exxon is trading like more of a hybrid of utility/growth, and there is not a significant cushion for deep value at the moment. For this reason, I think it would be perfectly acceptable for investors to wait for more of a decline before buying XOM stock. In any case, Chevron Corporation (CVX) is trading at more expensive levels (both in terms of price to earnings and EV/EBITDA), so I don’t think it would make sense to refer to Exxon as being overpriced right now, either. For income investors looking for a “sweet spot”, Exxon’s valuation seems to be an acceptable trade-off to achieve predictable dividend coverage without having to pay a premium (which would be the case for CVX).

Exxon's ability to generate returns on capital employed also leads the pack, but I guess the elephant in the room would have to be the comparatively “low” dividend yield of 3.35%. I suppose every investor will have to decide for themselves whether raw payout percentages should be prized above stability. But if we look at the relative payout ratios of these three stocks, I think there are some clear warning signs that should be mentioned. At 57.5%, Exxon’s payout ratio should be characterized as being covered in a way that’s durable. I see tremendous risks with BP’s payout ratio of nearly 320%. Of course, Chevron’s position is not nearly as concerning (at 95.4%) but I do think that Exxon is very underrated here in terms of the stock’s income stability. If the macroeconomic environment starts to soften up (which is likely to occur, in my view), stocks like BP (and maybe even Chevron) could encounter risks of a dividend cut in the first half of 2026.

So while I cannot fully say that XOM has been a “star performer” in my portfolio over the last six months, there is still a strong set of positives that should be considered by potential income investors. Since the publication of my last article, Exxon has been trading in a sideways trading range that has been suspiciously tight for most of the last six months. From a chart perspective, this can cause problems if range support starts to break and I am currently defining this region based on the price low from April 7th, 2025 (sometimes referred to as the Liberation Day low), which is seen at $97.80. As a result, I am viewing this area as a favorable “buy zone” for long entries if we start to see a larger dip in share prices. A downside break of this support region could lead to deeper declines, however, because we would actually have to look all the way back to the September 2022 lows of $83.89 in order to find the next level of historical support.

For these reasons, I would view any drop into roughly $85 to be an excellent buying opportunity for patient income investors. Downside price moves like this could also send Exxon’s forward PE ratio into more attractive territory in relation to the rest of the peer group, so any disruptions that might be caused by a short-term break of trading range support might actually turn out to be the preferred scenario. With all of this in mind, I will be revising my outlook to a “buy” rating based on the fact that I believe some degree of patience is warranted before adding to XOM long positions but that the stock’s superior dividend stability still places Exxon in an attractive position regarding the rest of the oil industry.

data.title: CoreWeave's Sell-Off Hides The Real Story
data.description:
The market reacted sharply as shares slid following guidance for CoreWeave’s (CRWV) softer top-line performance, despite strong double-digit growth and a backlog that surged to $55.6 billion. Investors misread a temporary powered-shell delay, caused by a data-center supplier, as a deeper structural issue. In reality, the holdup simply shifts capacity delivery rather than undermining CoreWeave’s execution.

The facility, complete with GPUs, power, and cooling, will come online later than planned, but contracts remain intact. According to the CFO, the delay merely pushes several hundred million dollars of revenue into early 2026, extending the contract timeline without altering its overall value. CRWV is down 13% from my earlier analysis, and I see the correction as a healthy setup for a strong rebound.

Lastly, the planned all-stock acquisition deal worth approximately $9 billion involving Core Scientific (CORZ) and CoreWeave collapsed after Core Scientific's shareholders rejected the deal, attributed to low share value due to a decrease in CoreWeave's share value. This signals that CoreWeave is being cautious about taking on much leverage and overpaying, while being more selective about scaling out its infrastructure.

The Physics of AI Supply Chains

A powered shell is like a move-in-ready building for data centers, the structure, electrical systems, and cooling are complete, but the servers haven’t been installed yet. CoreWeave leases these from third-party developers instead of building them. One of those developers fell behind because it couldn’t get the new site connected to the local power grid.

That grid connection, controlled by utilities already overwhelmed by surging AI-related demand, caused months of delay. As a result, CoreWeave couldn’t activate servers or recognize revenue until 2026, frustrating investors who feared more delays and higher financing costs might ripple through future projects.

In today’s world, the real question isn’t just this one building that got pushed out, but what the physical limitations are of how far you can extend this infrastructure with AI. The lagging project by CoreWeave hints at a point that all hyperscalers, including AWS and Azure, are fighting for the same limited number of developers and partners to erect their data centers compatible with GPUs. The building and the power infrastructure are lagging in readiness to wire up the GPUs, a condition that puts all AI clouds under strain as it's a question of building out power and cooling infrastructure at a pace we've never seen before.

To mitigate, CoreWeave has already diversified their portfolio into over 30 facilities and involved multiple developers. The company saw an increase in their operational power to 590 MW in Q3, an improvement of 120 MW, while contracted power reached 2.9 GW. The delay will have an effect on less than 5% of their market, and once operational, this will directly contribute to their bottom-line growth, proving that they are past the scale physics issue, rather than a concern related to market demands.

CoreWeave’s Delays Spooked The Market But Not The Fundamentals

Where the market may be absolutely correct is in their concern with regard to timing sensitivity. A company so capital-intensive does not allow for much room for error. Every quarter that passes pushes the breakeven point out even further, which may try the patience and convictions of investors and banks, among others, respectively. It does not, however, necessarily render the model put forth by CoreWeave incorrect, as this company exists at a nexus between the world of finance and physics, respectively.

In Q3, its interest expense budget was approximately equal to adjusted operating income. Naturally, this makes a delay, even a small one, all the harder to sustain. The delay was seen as a negative signal by investors, but this should be kept in perspective as all contracts are ‘take-or-pay,’ so customers pay whether utilization rates are high or low once the capacity comes online. The delay puts none of this money at risk; it is all locked in, rather than at risk, as a result of the delay.

CoreWeave’s Q3 backlog surge tells a clearer story than the market downturn implied. The company ended the quarter with $55.6 billion in revenue backlog, up 271% year-over-year and nearly double quarter-over-quarter, driven by major multi-year agreements with OpenAI, Meta, and several hyperscalers. The chart shows that about 40% of this backlog is set to convert within 24 months, giving CoreWeave unusually strong near-term revenue visibility. At the same time, the duration mix broadened across all time frames, reflecting deep, multi-cycle demand.

Quality is improving as well, with 60% of backlog now coming from investment-grade customers, while top-customer concentration has fallen from 85% to 35% over the past year. This combination of scale and diversification highlights structural strengthening rather than risk. The backlog chart ultimately illustrates how CoreWeave is expanding faster than the physical infrastructure can be delivered, a timing issue the market misread, not a deterioration in demand.

As a result, CoreWeave added to its debt facility to fuel its aggressive growth strategy to support its growing $55 billion total backlog of revenues and its growing infrastructure presence overall. The modified DDTL 3.0 debt facility adds $3 billion of new capital at a favorable spread of SOFR +4.0-4.25%, below higher spreads offered on preceding facilities for each tranche of total growth to date. This further asserts lenders' confidence in its take-or-pay contract business approach overall.

The company expects this additional leverage to moderately increase annual interest expense in 2026 from its current $1.21–1.25 billion range as it ramps up the use of newly expanded lending facilities. Management anticipates margins and EBITDA growth to stabilize in 2027 as these capacity investments begin contributing to revenue at scale.

The Only Hyper-Growth Cloud Trading At A Value Multiple

CRWV’s valuation is attractive when compared to its closest peers within high-growth infrastructure. CoreWeave is currently valued at 6.61x price/sales, which is significantly lower than its peers such as NBIS at 53.3x, CIFR at 25.7x, and WULF at 25.6x. Compared on a forward EV/sales multiple, 10.8x is significantly more reasonable than NBIS at 36x, WULF at 27x, and CIFR at 21x, which is rather astonishing when one takes into account CoreWeave’s triple-digit revenue growth.

The EV/EBITDA ratio is consistent with this mismatch. The EV/EBITDA ratio at CoreWeave of 25.4x is significantly lower than that of CIFR at 65x and WULF at 110x, while having much better visibility into backlog and quality customers. The company’s P/B ratio at 9.9x is also relatively low compared to peers, reflecting its contracted revenue base and rapid addition of new capacity.

A glance at this table indicates that CoreWeave is significantly cheaper compared to those that have slower growth, have weaker customer profiles, and much less revenue visibility. This helps to show that the sentiment-driven selloff that occurred is not fundamentally justified.

Risks And The Road Ahead

A rebound is already apparent in the 2026 outlook presented by CoreWeave, as the company expects their CapEx to top $25 billion, a number well over double this year, with an end 850MW operating their power by 2025. Delayed projects starting operation will bring revenue recognition, hence better conversion rates and EBITDA margins. On the other hand, the self-building activities by CoreWeave in both Pennsylvania and Europe will ensure a reduced dependency on third-party developers as well.

As a result, by involving itself further in the supply chain, the company will acquire better control over delivery, cost, and reliability, ultimately managing to change the same risk that scared investors into a moat. The battleground that CoreWeave faces is execution with leverage.

Bottom Line

The sell-off wasn’t a result of a failing company, but a misinterpreted timeline. The “delay” at CoreWeave is a symptom of hyperscaling versus the physical limitations inherent in AI infrastructure, rather than a problem with the company’s foundation.

The money hasn’t been lost, just pushed forward. Contracts are unaffected. The market continues to outstrip supply. It will be viewed, in the end, as a confirmation, rather than a cautionary tale, that CoreWeave is indeed growing far faster than the world can build to keep up with them.
